title: W2 B Elements Topology
body:
Mathematical Economics

Juan Carlos Carbajal

School of Economics UNSW–Sydney

Week 2B — Term 1, 2026

1/12   Continuous Preferences

If you have taken a microeconomics course, you know this figure.

good 2

N W T (x)

x I(x) NBT (x) good 1

2/12   Continuous Preferences

If you have taken a microeconomics course, you know this figure.

good 2

N W T (x)

x I(x) NBT (x) good 1

◦ I(x) is the set of consumption bundles that are indifferent to x. ◦ N W T (x) is the set of bundles that are no-worse-than-x. ◦ NBT (x) is the set of bundles that are no-better-than-x.

Why is this figure like that?

2/12   Continuous Preferences

Important features of the previous figure respond to an assumption on individual preferences.

Definition 1

Complete and transitive preference ⪰ defined on X = Rn + are continuous if for every pair of bundles x, y in X, with x ≻ y, there exists some ϵ > 0 such that if x′ is less than ϵ distant from x and y ′ is less than ϵ distant from y, one has x′ ≻ y ′ .

In words, if x is strictly preferred to y, any bundle sufficiently close to x should also be strictly preferred to any bundle sufficiently close to y.

3/12   Distance Representation

4/12  Elements of Topology

5/12

The distance between two real numbers a, b ∈ R is |a − b| ≥ 0.

6/12

The distance between two real numbers a, b ∈ R is |a − b| ≥ 0.

The distance between a = (a1 , a2 ) and b = (b1 , b2 ) in R2 is . . .

x2 b

a x1

6/12

Candidates for distance in R2 : ◦ |a1 − b1 | + |a2 − b2 |.

x2 b

a x1

7/12

Candidates for distance in R2 : ◦ |a1 − b1 | + |a2 − b2 |.

x2 b

a x1

◦ max {|a1 − b1 |, |a2 − b2 |}.

What would be the ideal properties of any distance?

7/12

Definition 2

Let S be a non-empty set. A distance (or metric) is a function d : S × S → R satisfying the following properties: for all s, t, u ∈ S, (M1) d(s, t) ≥ 0, with equality if and only if s = t;

(M2) d(s, t) = d(t, s);

(M3) d(s, u) ≤ d(s, t) + d(t, u).

8/12

Definition 2

Let S be a non-empty set. A distance (or metric) is a function d : S × S → R satisfying the following properties: for all s, t, u ∈ S, (M1) d(s, t) ≥ 0, with equality if and only if s = t;

(M2) d(s, t) = d(t, s);

(M3) d(s, u) ≤ d(s, t) + d(t, u).

Definition 3

A metric space is a set S together with a metric d : S × S → R.

Note that a set S can be endowed with difference metrics.

8/12   Examples of Metric Spaces

Example 1

R together with dE (x, y) = |x − y| constitute a metric space.

9/12   Examples of Metric Spaces

Example 1

R together with dE (x, y) = |x − y| constitute a metric space.

Example 2

Define dD : R × R → R by  0 if x = y, dD (x, y) = 1 if x ̸= y.

9/12   Examples of Metric Spaces

Example 1

R together with dE (x, y) = |x − y| constitute a metric space.

Example 2

Define dD : R × R → R by  0 if x = y, dD (x, y) = 1 if x ̸= y.

To verify that the discrete metric is a distance function in R, we need to verify that (M1) dD (x, y) ≥ 0 with equality if and only if x = y; (M2) dD (x, y) = dD (y, x); and (M3) dD (x, z) ≤ dD (x, y) + dD (y, z).

9/12   Euclidean Distance in Rn

Definition 4

The Euclidean distance dE : Rn × Rn → R is defined by n X 1 dE (x, y) = (xi − yi )2 . i=1

This is usually denoted by ∥x − y∥.

10/12   Euclidean Distance in Rn

Definition 4

The Euclidean distance dE : Rn × Rn → R is defined by n X 1 dE (x, y) = (xi − yi )2 . i=1

This is usually denoted by ∥x − y∥.

Clearly: (M1) ∥x − y∥ ≥ 0, with equality if and only if x = y.

(M2) ∥x − y∥ = ∥y − x∥.

10/12   Euclidean Distance in Rn

Definition 4

The Euclidean distance dE : Rn × Rn → R is defined by n X 1 dE (x, y) = (xi − yi )2 . i=1

This is usually denoted by ∥x − y∥.

Clearly: (M1) ∥x − y∥ ≥ 0, with equality if and only if x = y.

(M2) ∥x − y∥ = ∥y − x∥.

Verifying that the triangle inequality (M3) holds is more involved.

10/12

Xn i=1

Xn i=1 X X X = x2i + 2 xi yi + yi2 i i i

Xn i=1 X X X = x2i + 2 xi yi + yi2 i i i

≤ ∥x∥2 + 2 |x · y| + ∥y∥2

Xn i=1 X X X = x2i + 2 xi yi + yi2 i i i

≤ ∥x∥2 + 2 |x · y| + ∥y∥2

≤ ∥x∥2 + 2 ∥x∥ ∥y∥ + ∥y∥2 = (∥x∥ + ∥y∥)2 .

Using Lemma 1, it is straightforward to show that the Euclidean distance dE satisfies (M3): for any x, y, z ∈ Rn

dE (x, z) = ∥x − z∥ = ∥(x − y) + (y − z)∥ ≤ ∥(x − y)∥ + ∥(y − z)∥ = dE (x, y) + dE (y, z).

◦ The second equality uses the fact that 0 = y − y and w + 0 = w, for any w in Rn . ◦ The inequality follows from Lemma 1, since both (x − y) and (y − z) are vectors in Rn .

12/12
